svg.title: MN 47 Vīmaṁsaka Sutta — Examining the Teacher
svg.desc: A discourse visualization of MN 47: how a disciple who cannot read minds should examine the Tathāgata through sight and hearing across six progressive levels of behavior, integrity, and teaching to establish unshakeable, reason-based faith.

g.text: “One who is an inquirer, not knowing how to encompass another’s mind, should examine a Teacher...”
vīmaṁsakena bhikkhunā parassa cetopariyāyaṁ ajānantena tathāgate samannesanā kātabbā
TWO FIELDS OF INQUIRY · DVĪSU DHAMMESU
“What qualities of the teacher are evident through what is seen and heard?”
A six-point checklist for examining a teacher
Check for any corrupt, defiled traits of mind shown in actions or speech.
Check for mixed or inconsistent habits alternating between clean and compromised.
Check whether wholesome, completely clean qualities are visible in behavior.
Check whether the state is long-standing and integrated, not a recent phase.
Check whether fame and a large following bring drawbacks such as conceit or arrogance.
Check whether restraint comes from fearlessness and the wearing away of passion.
Cross-verifying a teacher’s conduct in community and in solitude against their own testimony
Whether dwelling in the Saṅgha or alone
Behaves with no conceit, despising none
“Are there found in the Teacher defiled, muddled, or purified mental qualities?”
“Purified mental qualities are found in me.”
“They are my path, my domain—yet I do not identify with them”
How direct knowledge arises through progressively refined teachings

text.tspan: with its higher and refined levels,

g.text: The Teacher is perfectly awakened · The Dhamma is well-proclaimed · The Saṅgha is practicing well
“Such faith is supported by reasons, rooted in understanding...
unshakeable by any ascetic, brahmin, deity, Māra, Brahmā, or anyone else
